<sentence>Common terns exhibit a migratory pattern that spans vast distances, traveling between breeding grounds in temperate regions and overwintering sites in tropical or subtropical areas.</sentence>
<sentence>Their seasonal movements are primarily driven by the availability of food resources, particularly small fish, crustaceans, and insects.</sentence>
<sentence>During the breeding season, typically from spring to summer, common terns congregate in colonies along coastlines, islands, and freshwater lakes.</sentence>
<sentence>These colonies can be quite large, offering protection in numbers against predators and facilitating social interactions for mating rituals.</sentence>
<sentence>As autumn approaches, a shift in behavior occurs, signaling the beginning of their long southward migration.</sentence>
<sentence>They often travel in flocks, though individual variation in migration timing and routes can be observed.</sentence>
<sentence>The journey south can take them across continents and oceans, utilizing prevailing winds and oceanic currents to their advantage.</sentence>
<sentence>Upon reaching their overwintering grounds, they disperse along coastlines and estuaries, continuing to forage for food.</sentence>
<sentence>With the onset of spring, the urge to breed draws them back north, completing the annual cycle.</sentence>
<sentence>This predictable yet extensive pattern is a testament to their adaptability and the rich ecological resources of both their summer and winter habitats.</sentence>
